key_insight: Adjoint methods and PINNs are not universally better than one another; the best choice depends mainly on how the unknown is represented, with grid-based fields favoring adjoints and neural representations favoring PINNs.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained inverse problems in mechanics and fluids, this paper gives a fair baseline for choosing between classical adjoint optimization and PINNs under matched problem settings.
method: Common formulation with matched domains, PDEs, observations, regularization, optimizer, parameterization, and precision
Benchmark comparison on Burgers, Darcy permeability, Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and Navier--Stokes viscosity identification
PINN-warm-started adjoint strategy to combine fast neural initialization with high-accuracy adjoint refinement
evidence_signal: The abstract reports four benchmark inverse problems and compares reconstruction quality and cost under identical settings across both methods.
watch_item: Results depend strongly on the chosen parameterization and can be affected by memory/trajectory-storage costs in time-dependent adjoint runs.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
